perturbation.original_text: Payments shall be made 15 days from the end of each quarter.
perturbation.changed_text: Payments shall be made 60 days from the end of each quarter.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment timeline to 60 days after the end of the quarter introduces a delay that may not comply with state laws regarding timely payment for services rendered.
perturbation.law_explanation: Arizona law mandates specific timeframes for wage payments. While this agreement doesn't explicitly cover 'wages,' the royalty payments for services could be interpreted as such, and a 60-day delay might violate the spirit of the law requiring timely compensation.

The official version of the Arizona Revised Statutes is published by Thomson Reuters. ARS Title Page Previous Section Title 23 Next Section Printable Version 23-353 - Payment of wages of discharged employee; violation; classification 23-353 . Payment of wages of discharged employee; violation; classification A. When an employee is discharged from the service of an employer, he shall be paid wages due him within seven working days or the end of the next regular pay period, whichever is sooner. B. When an employee quits the service of an employer he shall be paid in the usual manner all wages due him no later than the regular payday for the pay period during which the termination occurred. If requested by the employee, such wages shall be paid by mail. C. Every employer, including this state and its political subdivisions, shall pay wages or compensation due an employee under this section in lawful money of the United States by negotiable check, draft, money order or warrant, in the case of the state or any political subdivision, which can be immediately redeemed in cash at a bank or other financial institution, payable on demand or by deposit in a financial institution of employee's choice and dated not later than the day upon which the check, draft, money order or warrant is given, and not otherwise. D. A person violating this section is guilty of a petty offense. perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the payment timeline to 60 days from the end of the quarter, while Arizona Revised Statutes (ARS) 23-353 A mandates that a discharged employee be paid wages due within seven working days or the end of the next regular pay period, whichever is sooner. While the contract language concerns royalty payments, the law explanation suggests these payments could be interpreted as wages for services, making the 60-day delay potentially non-compliant with timely wage payment requirements under Arizona law.

perturbation.original_text: Smith agrees that any material, advertising or otherwise, submitted for approval as provided herein may be deemed by TDA to have been approved hereunder if the same is not disapproved in writing within fourteen (14) days after receipt thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Smith agrees that any material, advertising or otherwise, submitted for approval as provided herein may be deemed by TDA to have been approved hereunder if the same is not disapproved in writing within three (3) days after receipt thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the approval timeframe to 3 days puts undue pressure on Smith, potentially conflicting with reasonable business practice regulations for fair contract terms.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly a fraud statute, the Arizona Consumer Fraud Act prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices in trade or commerce. A court might interpret a 3-day approval window as an unfair practice if it doesn't allow reasonable time for review, especially given that Smith is the talent.

Previous Next 44-1522. Unlawful practices; intended interpretation of provisions A. The act, use or employment by any person of any deception, deceptive or unfair act or practice, fraud, false pretense, false promise, misrepresentation, or concealment, suppression or omission of any material fact with intent that others rely on such concealment, suppression or omission, in connection with the sale or advertisement of any merchandise whether or not any person has in fact been misled, deceived or damaged thereby, is declared to be an unlawful practice. B. The violation of chapter 9, article 16 or chapter 19, article 1 of this title is declared to be an unlawful practice and subject to enforcement under this article. C. It is the intent of the legislature, in construing subsection A, that the courts may use as a guide interpretations given by the federal trade commission and the federal courts to 15 United States Code sections 45, 52 and 55(a)(1). perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the approval timeframe from 14 days to 3 days, which the perturbation explanation argues places undue pressure on Smith and may conflict with fair contract terms. The law explanation suggests this timeframe could be interpreted as an unfair practice under the Arizona Consumer Fraud Act. Scraped Law Snippet 2, Arizona Revised Statutes § 44-1522, defines as an unlawful practice 'any deception, deceptive or unfair act or practice...in connection with the sale or advertisement of any merchandise'. The shortened approval period could be deemed an unfair act or practice, making the contract non-compliant, supporting a 'YES' answer.

perturbation.original_text: All books of account and records of Licensee covering all transactions relating to the Licensee shall be retained by the Licensee until at least two (2) years after the expiration or termination of the Term for possible inspection by Smith.
perturbation.changed_text: All books of account and records of Licensee covering all transactions relating to the Licensee shall be retained by the Licensee until at least one (1) year after the expiration or termination of the Term for possible inspection by Smith.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the record retention period to one year may conflict with standard accounting practices and legal requirements for audits and potential disputes.
perturbation.law_explanation: Federal tax law (specifically IRC 6501) generally requires taxpayers to keep records for at least three years for audit purposes. While this is a contract between two private parties, reducing the record retention period to one year could impede Smith's ability to verify royalty payments and raise potential tax issues should the IRS decide to audit TDA's records.

Limitations on assessment and collection (a) General rule Except as otherwise provided in this section, the amount of any tax imposed by this title shall be assessed within 3 years after the return was filed (whether or not such return was filed on or after the date prescribed) or, if the tax is payable by stamp, at any time aft
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 26 U.S. Code § 6501 - Limitations on assessment and collection | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 26 U.S. Code § 6501 - Limitations on assessment and collection U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) General rule Except as otherwise provided in this section, the amount of any tax imposed by this title shall be assessed within 3 years after the return was filed (whether or not such return was filed on or after the date prescribed) or, if the tax is payable by stamp, at any time after such tax became due and before the expiration of 3 years after the date on which any part of such tax was paid, and no proceeding in court without assessment for the collection of such tax shall be begun after the expiration of such period. For purposes of this chapter, the term “ return ” means the return required to be filed by the taxpayer (and does not include a return of any person from whom the taxpayer has received an item of income, gain, loss, deduction, or credit). (b) Time return deemed filed (1) Early return For purposes of this section, a return of tax imposed by this title, except tax imposed by chapter 3, 4, 21, or 24, filed before the last day prescribed by law or by regulations promulgated pursuant to law for the filing thereof, shall be considered as filed on such last day. (2) Return of certain employment and withholding taxes For purposes of this section, if a return of tax imposed by chapter 3, 4, 21, or 24 for any period ending with or within a calendar year is filed before April 15 of the succeeding calendar year, such return shall be considered filed on April 15 of such calendar year. (3) Return executed by Secretary Notwithstanding the provisions of paragraph (2) of section 6020(b), the execution of a return by the Secretary pursuant to the authority conferred by such section shall not start the running of the period of limitations on assessment and collectio
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the record retention period from two years to one year. The Law Explanation says that federal tax law (IRC 6501) generally requires taxpayers to keep records for at least three years for audit purposes, and that the changed text could impede Smith's ability to verify royalty payments and raise potential tax issues. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and Scraped Law Snippet 2 both reference 26 USC 6501, stating that the amount of any tax imposed by this title shall be assessed within 3 years after the return was filed. The changed text, by only requiring a one-year retention period, directly contradicts this law, since the records would not be available for the full three-year assessment period. Therefore, a legal contradiction exists because the contract now violates federal tax law.
